parts.prompt:
An incomplete mass spectrum for magnesium is shown in the diagram.
The percent abundance of magnesium-24 is 79%. The percent abundances of the other two
natural isotopes of magnesium, magnesium-25 and magnesium-26, are approximately equal.
i. Complete the mass spectrum in part A by drawing thick lines in the appropriate locations
to represent the percent abundance of magnesium-25 and magnesium-26.
ii. Describe the difference in atomic structure that accounts for the difference in mass
between magnesium-25 and magnesium-26.

A student prepares a 1.85× 10$^{-3}$ M solution of Mg($NO_3$ ) 2 (aq) in beaker 1 and a 2.80× 10$^{-4}$ M
solution of
NaOH(aq)
in beaker 2, as shown.
Mg2+
parts.scoring_notes: For part (a)(i): each line for Mg-25 and Mg-26 should appear at roughly 10–11% abundance. For part (a)(ii): accept any response stating that Mg-26 has one more neutron than Mg-25.

scoring_points.description: For a correct description of the structural difference between Mg-25 and Mg-26: 1 point

required_elements: Magnesium-26 has one more neutron than magnesium-25.
alternatives.correct_example: Magnesium-26 has one more neutron in its nucleus than magnesium-25. Both isotopes have 12 protons and 12 electrons; the difference is one additional neutron in Mg-26.

wrong_examples: Saying Mg-26 has one more proton — proton number defines the element and cannot differ between isotopes of the same element.
common_traps: Confusing isotopes with ions; isotopes differ only in neutron number, not proton or electron number.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 02: For the correct answer: Magnesium-26 has one more neutron than magnesium-25 does.

sample_responses.response: Magnesium-26 has one more neutron than magnesium-25. Both isotopes have 12 protons and 12 electrons; they differ only in neutron count (Mg-25 has 13 neutrons, Mg-26 has 14 neutrons).
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the structural difference as one additional neutron in Mg-26 compared to Mg-25. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Magnesium-26 has one more proton than magnesium-25.
sample_responses.commentary: Proton number defines the element — both are magnesium with 12 protons. The mass difference between isotopes comes from different neutron counts. No credit.

parts.prompt:
The particle diagram shown represents a magnesium ion, , in beaker 1. A sodium
Na+ Mg2+
ion, , in beaker 2 has a weaker attraction to water than the does. Explain this
phenomenon using Coulomb's law and each of the following.
i. The relative charge of the ions
ii. The relative radii of the ions
parts.scoring_notes: A complete response must address both charge and radius using Coulomb's law. Either element alone is insufficient for the point.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation using Coulomb's law referencing both charge and radius: 1 point

required_elements: Na⁺ has a smaller charge (+1) than Mg²⁺ (+2), so the electrostatic attraction to water is weaker.
Na⁺ has a larger ionic radius than Mg²⁺, increasing the distance between ion and water dipole, further reducing the attraction per Coulomb's law ($F \propto \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$).
alternatives.correct_example: According to Coulomb's law, $F \propto \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$. The Na⁺ ion has a charge of +1 while Mg²⁺ has a charge of +2, so the numerator is smaller for Na⁺. Additionally, Na⁺ has a larger ionic radius than Mg²⁺ (Na⁺ is in period 3 and has lost only one electron, while Mg²⁺ has lost two), so the distance $r$ is larger for Na⁺. Both factors reduce the force of attraction of Na⁺ for water compared to Mg²⁺.

wrong_examples: Stating only that Na⁺ has a smaller charge without mentioning radius, or vice versa.
common_traps: Students often claim Na⁺ is smaller than Mg²⁺ — this is incorrect; Na⁺ (102 pm) is larger than Mg²⁺ (72 pm).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 03: For a correct explanation: The charge on the sodium ion is less than the charge on the magnesium ion. A smaller charge results in a weaker Coulombic attraction between Na+ and water. Point 04: For a correct explanation: The Na+ ion is larger than the Mg2+ ion, so the distance between the Na+ and the oxygen on the water molecule will be greater. As distance increases, Coulombic attraction decreases.

sample_responses.response: According to Coulomb's law, $F \propto q_1 q_2 / r^2$. Na⁺ has a +1 charge vs. +2 for Mg²⁺, so the numerator (charge product) is smaller for Na⁺, giving a weaker attraction to water.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses Coulomb's law and correctly compares ionic charges — smaller charge on Na⁺ means weaker electrostatic attraction to the polar water molecule. Earns one of the two sub-points.

sample_responses.response: Na⁺ is a larger ion than Mg²⁺, so the distance between Na⁺ and the water molecule's oxygen is greater. By Coulomb's law, larger distance means weaker attraction.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the distance relationship in Coulomb's law. Na⁺ (102 pm) is larger than Mg²⁺ (72 pm), increasing the ion-dipole distance and weakening the force. Earns the second sub-point.

sample_responses.response: Sodium is less reactive than magnesium, so it interacts less with water.
sample_responses.commentary: Reactivity is not a Coulomb's law argument. The question requires an explanation based on charge and ionic radius. No credit.

parts.prompt: Calculate the pH of the solution in beaker 2.
parts.scoring_notes: Sig figs: 2 sig figs acceptable (10.4). Units not required for pH.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated pH value: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using $\text{pH} = -\log(2.80 \times 10^{-4})$ directly — this gives pOH, not pH.
common_traps: Forgetting NaOH is a strong base and fully dissociates, so $[\text{OH}^-] = 2.80 \times 10^{-4}$ M directly.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 05: For the correct calculated value: pOH = −log(2.80 × 10⁻⁴) = 3.553; pH = 14 − pOH = 14 − 3.553 = 10.447.

sample_responses.response: NaOH is a strong base, so $[\text{OH}^-] = 2.80 \times 10^{-4}$ M. $\text{pOH} = -\log(2.80 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.553$. $\text{pH} = 14 - 3.553 = 10.45$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies NaOH as a strong base, computes pOH from [OH⁻], and converts to pH using the water autoionization relationship. Earns the point.
This calculates pOH, not pH. For a basic solution, pH > 7. The student must convert pOH to pH using pH + pOH = 14. No credit.

parts.prompt:
A student combines 35.00 mL of 1.85× 10$^{-3}$ M Mg($NO_3$ ) 2 (aq) with 50.00 mL of
2.80× 10$^{-4}$
M
NaOH(aq),
as shown in the diagram. Calculate
Mg2+
after the two solutions are
combined but before any reaction takes place. (Assume that volumes are additive.)
parts.scoring_notes: Accept equivalent setups using dilution formula $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated [Mg²⁺] after mixing: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using only the original volume (35.00 mL) in the denominator instead of the total combined volume.
common_traps: Forgetting that Mg(NO₃)₂ dissociates to give Mg²⁺, but concentration of Mg²⁺ equals concentration of the salt (1:1 ratio).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 06: For the correct calculated value, consistent with part D: $M_2 = (1.85 \times 10^{-3}\text{ M})(0.03500\text{ L}) \div (0.03500\text{ L} + 0.05000\text{ L}) = 7.62 \times 10^{-4}\text{ M}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Applies dilution formula using total combined volume (85.00 mL) in the denominator. Earns the point.
Uses only the original volume in the denominator, not accounting for dilution when the two solutions are combined. The total volume must be used. No credit.

parts.prompt:
The dissolution of magnesium hydroxide is represented by the following equation.
$Mg(OH)_2$(s) E Mg2+(aq)+ 2 $OH^-$(aq) $K_{sp}$ = 5.61× 10$^{-12}$
i. Write the expression for the solubility product constant, $K_{sp}$.
ii. After the two solutions are combined in beaker 3 as described in part D, but before any
reaction takes place, $OH^-$ = 1.65× 10$^{-4}$ M. Using your answer to part D, calculate the
value of the reaction quotient, Q.
iii. Using the reaction quotient, Q, predict whether a precipitate should form as the mixture
in beaker 3 approaches equilibrium. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (e)(i): accept equivalent correct expression. Part (e)(ii): must use [Mg²⁺] from part (d) (or consistent value). Part (e)(iii): accept any correctly reasoned comparison of Q to Ksp.

scoring_points.description: For the correct $K_{sp}$ expression, Q calculation, and precipitate prediction: 1 point

required_elements: $Q > K_{sp}$ (2.07 × 10⁻¹¹ > 5.61 × 10⁻¹²), so a precipitate will form.
alternatives.correct_example:
$K_{sp} = [\text{Mg}^{2+}][\text{OH}^-]^2$ 
$Q = (7.62 \times 10^{-4})(1.65 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 2.07 \times 10^{-11}$ 
Since $Q = 2.07 \times 10^{-11} > K_{sp} = 5.61 \times 10^{-12}$, the solution is supersaturated and Mg(OH)₂ will precipitate.

wrong_examples: Writing $K_{sp} = [\text{Mg}^{2+}]^2[\text{OH}^-]$ — exponent is on OH⁻, not Mg²⁺.
Concluding no precipitate when Q > Ksp.
common_traps: Forgetting to square [OH⁻] in the Q expression.
Confusing the direction of Q vs. Ksp comparison.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 07: For the correct expression: Ksp = [Mg²⁺][OH⁻]². Point 08: For the correct calculated value consistent with parts D and E(i): Q = [Mg²⁺][OH⁻]² = (7.62 × 10⁻⁴)(1.65 × 10⁻⁴)² = 2.07 × 10⁻¹¹. Point 09: For the correct prediction and justification: Q > Ksp, so a precipitate will form.

sample_responses.response:
$K_{sp} = [\text{Mg}^{2+}][\text{OH}^-]^2$ 
$Q = (7.62 \times 10^{-4})(1.65 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 2.07 \times 10^{-11}$ 
Since $Q = 2.07 \times 10^{-11} > K_{sp} = 5.61 \times 10^{-12}$, the solution is supersaturated and \ce{Mg(OH)2} will precipitate.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly writes the Ksp expression with [OH⁻]², calculates Q using the diluted [Mg²⁺] from part d, and compares Q > Ksp to predict precipitation. All three sub-points earned.
The exponents are swapped — [OH⁻] has the coefficient of 2 in the dissolution equation (one Mg²⁺ and two OH⁻), so the expression must be [Mg²⁺][OH⁻]². No credit.

parts.prompt:
In a separate experiment, the student adds HNO 3 (aq) to decrease the pH of a saturated
solution containing undissolved $Mg(OH)_2$ (s). Does the amount of undissolved $Mg(OH)_2$ (s)
increase, decrease, or remain the same as the HNO 3 (aq) is added? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any correct Le Châtelier or Q vs. Ksp reasoning.

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer (decrease) and a valid Le Châtelier/Q justification: 1 point

required_elements: H⁺ from HNO₃ reacts with OH⁻, decreasing [OH⁻] and causing Q < Ksp, shifting equilibrium toward dissolution of more Mg(OH)₂(s).
alternatives.correct_example: Decrease. The H⁺ ions from HNO₃(aq) react with OH⁻(aq) according to $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, consuming OH⁻ and lowering [OH⁻]. This makes Q < Ksp, so more Mg(OH)₂(s) dissolves to reestablish equilibrium, resulting in less undissolved solid.

wrong_examples: Saying the amount increases — HNO₃ promotes dissolution by removing OH⁻, not precipitation.
common_traps: Confusing the effect of adding acid to a sparingly soluble base salt — it always increases solubility.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 10: For the correct answer and justification: Decrease. The H⁺ from HNO₃(aq) will react with OH⁻, decreasing [OH⁻] and causing Q < Ksp. As a result, more Mg(OH)₂(s) will dissolve until equilibrium is reestablished, resulting in less Mg(OH)₂(s).

sample_responses.response: The amount of undissolved \ce{Mg(OH)2(s)} will decrease. The \ce{H+} from \ce{HNO3} reacts with \ce{OH-} according to \ce{H+ + OH- -> H2O}, which decreases [\ce{OH-}]. This makes $Q < K_{sp}$, so the equilibrium shifts toward dissolving more \ce{Mg(OH)2(s)} — resulting in less solid remaining.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that H⁺ consumes OH⁻, lowers Q below Ksp, and shifts equilibrium toward dissolution. Exactly matches CB's stated justification. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The amount of undissolved \ce{Mg(OH)2(s)} increases because the acid will form a precipitate with the Mg²⁺ ions.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect prediction. Adding HNO₃ to Mg(OH)₂ promotes dissolution (not precipitation) by consuming OH⁻ ions. No acid-base precipitate forms. No credit.
